topic: Two colleagues are both considered for the same promotion
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed, a grating soundtrack to my internal unraveling. I stared at the email, the subject line a cruel, glittering shard: "Promotion Consideration." My hands were slick with sweat, and I could feel the frantic drumming beneath my ribs. Sarah had been mentioned first, and the air around her desk seemed to shimmer with unspoken approval. I paced the narrow aisle between the filing cabinets, my breath coming in shallow, ragged gasps. Every glance from a passing cubicle neighbor felt like a spotlight, dissecting my inadequacy. *It can’t be happening. It simply cannot be.* I gripped the edge of my desk until my knuckles were white, a desperate attempt to anchor myself against the swirling chaos in my chest.

[story 2]

"They're talking about me," I whispered, the sound barely audible above the distant keyboard clicks. My vision tunneled, the edges of the room blurring into streaks of beige and grey. I kept running my tongue over my dry lips, tasting the metallic tang of sheer panic. I snatched my phone, my fingers fumbling so badly the screen blurred. I needed proof, a tangible sign that reality hadn't dissolved into this dizzying, high-pitched ringing in my ears. When I finally looked up, the hallway seemed too wide, the silence too loud. My heart hammered against my sternum, a frantic trapped bird demanding release. I started shaking, a violent tremor that made the papers on my desk flutter like disturbed insects.

[story 3]

I couldn't focus on the quarterly reports. The numbers swam before my eyes, meaningless hieroglyphs mocking my sudden, intense distress. Every time Mark walked past, I felt a sharp, sickening lurch in my stomach, a wave of nausea so potent it made me press my palms against my eyes. I started talking, a rapid-fire stream of disjointed sentences about coffee, about deadlines, about the absurdity of it all. My voice cracked mid-word, high and thin, completely out of sync with my own thoughts. I needed to move, to escape the confines of this space, but my legs felt disconnected, heavy, as if submerged in thick syrup. I just stood there, breathing in sharp, shallow bursts, waiting for the floor to tilt beneath me.
